steps.prompt: Which is the main verb of the second clause?

hints.culpa: 'culpa' is a noun meaning 'fault' — it appears in the ablative phrase 'mea culpa'
hints.orare: 'orare' is an infinitive — it depends on 'precor', not the main finite verb itself

hints.to believe: 'to believe' is 'credere' — 'precari' means to pray or beseech
hints.to confess: 'to confess' is 'confiteri' — that was the verb in the first half

hints.2nd singular: 2nd singular deponent would be 'precaris' — the -or ending is 1st singular
hints.3rd singular: 3rd singular deponent would be 'precatur' — the -or ending is 1st person
hints.1st plural: 1st plural deponent would be 'precamur' — 'precor' has no plural marker

hints.Nominative: Nominative is the same form, but 'mea culpa' is an ablative of cause — 'through my fault'

hints.Positive: The positive degree is 'magna' — 'maxima' is the superlative of 'magnus'
hints.Comparative: The comparative is 'maior' — 'maxima' goes one step further to superlative
hints.Elative: Elative is an intensive use of the superlative — 'maxima' here is true superlative in the triple climax

steps.prompt: What is the rhetorical effect of 'mea culpa' repeated three times?

hints.Simple emphasis: It's more than simple emphasis — the addition of 'maxima' in the third creates escalation
hints.Enumeration of different faults: The same word 'culpa' is repeated, not different faults — it intensifies the single confession
hints.Antithesis: Antithesis contrasts opposites — this builds in the same direction toward 'maxima'

hints.Conjunction: Conjunctions join clauses — 'ideo' modifies the whole clause as a connective adverb
hints.Preposition: Prepositions govern nouns — 'ideo' doesn't take an object
hints.Pronoun: Pronouns replace nouns — 'ideo' is an adverb of cause/result

hints.Indirect command (ut + subj.): 'precor' can take ut + subjunctive, but here the infinitive 'orare' is used directly with 'vos'
hints.Ablative absolute: Ablative absolute uses noun+participle in ablative — this is acc.+inf.
hints.Purpose clause: Purpose clauses use ut/ne + subjunctive — 'orare' is an infinitive, not subjunctive

hints.Nominative: 'vos' as nominative would be the subject — here it's the object of 'precor' in the acc.+inf. construction
hints.Dative: Dative of 'vos' would also be 'vobis' — here 'vos' is accusative, subject of 'orare'

hints.Accusative: 'pro' always takes ablative — prepositions like 'per' take accusative
hints.Genitive: No standard Latin preposition governs genitive directly

translation: Through my fault, through my fault, through my most grievous fault; therefore I pray you, brothers, to pray for me
